insight: Shredder and Hannibull are most valuable as frontline soakers when your opponent cannot attack for exactly their HP values; if the opponent attacks for exactly 4, Shredder becomes a liability, but if they attack for 6 with a Wall absorber, Shredder provides great value.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion about when to buy and how to use frontline soak units like Shredder and Hannibull.

insight: When an enemy Shredder or Hannibull is blocking and you have enough damage to kill it, you should always do so; if you don't, your opponent retains the choice of whether to lose it, and they will always choose the outcome that benefits them most.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion about targeting decisions against frontline units.

insight: Attacking with a frontline unit like Hannibull instead of blocking is rarely worth it; spending 7 HP of tempo to deal 1 damage is not a good trade, so frontline units should generally be held back to soak.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about whether to click Hannibull or Shredder for attack versus defense.

insight: Shredder is effectively an Infusion Grid with 1 threat and extra abusability: the first Infusion Grid is better because it absorbs, but once a backline absorber is established, a Shredder bought for soak is equivalent to an Infusion Grid bought for soak plus added threat.
author: amalloy
context: Comparison of Shredder versus Infusion Grid as defensive units.

insight: In Prismata, 'soak' means sacrificing a unit on defense (losing it to absorb damage), while 'absorb' means redirecting damage to a unit that survives. Hannibull, Shredder, and Aegis all soak well; Aegis does not absorb.
author: amalloy
context: Clarification of soak vs. absorb terminology in a strategy discussion.

insight: As a rule of thumb, it is reasonable to hold 1 Scorchilla to deny 1 absorb, or 2 Scorchillas to deny 2 absorb; holding more than the absorb you are denying is wasteful.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion about when to hold Scorchillas back versus attacking, in the context of Plexo Cell absorb denial.

insight: Holding damage back to sync it does not add pressure unless it is denying absorb; if the opponent simply rebuilds the same defense they would have built anyway, holding damage is neutral at best.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion about syncing Immolites with Scorchillas and whether holding attack adds real pressure.

insight: Burst or synced damage is powerful because it is inefficient to pre-buy blockers before you need them; prompt blockers derive much of their value from being purchased exactly when needed, which is why Infusion Grid costs the same as Wall but has 1 more HP.
author: cogito3
context: Discussion about synced attackers and the value of burst damage versus steady pressure.

insight: When a Bombarder has only 1 stamina remaining, clicking it to attack does not increase your effective threat compared to holding it, so it is usually better to hold it and absorb on it next turn instead; exceptions are when it would kill a high-value unit (like a Rhino or Drone) or when you are already breaching.
author: cogito3
context: Discussion about the decision to click a 1-stamina Bombarder versus holding it for soak.

insight: Going high-econ (more drones) is correct when good absorb units are available in the set, because absorb reduces the efficiency of early Tarsiers and other attackers; without absorb, aggressive Tarsier rushes tend to beat greedy drone builds.
author: consensus
context: Discussion about when high-econ drone builds are justified versus aggressive early attack.

insight: In Zemora Voidbringer sets, P1 should typically open DD DC to contest the first Zemora rather than letting P2 get it first; P1 DD DC versus P2 DD DDC are roughly even, while P2 going DC DC is considered a bad opening.
author: extratricky
context: Discussion about correct Zemora set openings and the importance of contesting the first Zemora.

insight: With Zemora Voidbringer, you generally do not want to stockpile too much green before Zemora fires; aim for around 8-10 green for its first shot, then use the gold Zemora generates to build remaining Conduits over subsequent turns. Setting up Doomed Mech early to absorb before getting Asteri Cannon is the correct sequencing.
author: cogito3
context: Post-game analysis of a Zemora set where both players mishandled green economy timing.

insight: Apollo is considered quite weak overall; it is more expensive than comparable snipers like Cynestra, has buildtime 2, and is clunky to tech into. It only provides real value when hitting targets that are efficient to kill with it (e.g., Vivid Drones, Lucina) or preventing opponents from building specific units.
author: consensus
context: New player asked how to deal with Apollo; experts explained why it is actually weak.

insight: Against a Centurion, buying 3 Shiver Yetis to freeze it costs 15RRR while the Centurion costs 18GGBBR but provides 2 more attack; massing Shiver Yetis to stop Centurion is resource-inefficient for the Shiver player.
author: f300xen
context: Discussion about whether Centurion is good in Shiver Yeti sets.

insight: In blue/red sets with Hellhound, the opening DD DD DAB (Drone, Drone / Drone, Drone / Drone, Drone, Animus, Blastforge) is considered a strong and common P2 line, often better than alternatives like DD DB SA or DD DCC BA because it floats less gold and applies pressure faster.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of blue/red opening theory with Hellhound and Bloodrager in the set.

insight: Master Bot tends to overbuild drones, often purchasing significantly more than optimal; human players can exploit this by stopping at a lower drone count and transitioning to attack earlier.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy shared a game where he stopped at 11 drones while his human opponent went to 16, noting that Master Bot commonly makes the same over-droning mistake.

insight: Master Bot was observed buying a second Blastforge in a set that contained neither Drake nor Grenade Mech, which was noted as illogical tech acquisition with no apparent purpose.
author: _theavatar
context: Player observed Master Bot making an apparently irrational tech purchase during a game.

insight: In Prismata community usage, 'soak' refers to sacrificing a frontline unit on defense to absorb incoming damage, while 'absorb' refers to redirecting damage to a unit that survives the hit. These are distinct strategic concepts with different implications for defense planning.
author: amalloy
context: Terminology clarification after a new player confused soak and absorb.
